title: Isabel Rose: British woman jailed for blackmail after accusing banker of rape in Hong Kong
description: According to local press reports, Judge Adriana Tse sentenced her to five years in prison for blackmail and three years for perverting the course of j
body: According to local press reports, Judge Adriana Tse sentenced her to five years in prison for blackmail and three years for perverting the course of justice, with one year to be served consecutively. Prosecutors argued that Rose fabricated the allegation in an attempt to extort £100,000 ($134,000) from the man, whose identity was withheld. Rose denied the charges throughout the trial and has consistently maintained that she was raped.Rose had travelled to Hong Kong on 31 January 2024 after meeting the man while in Thailand a month earlier, according to court documents.At the centre of the prosecution case were WhatsApp messages exchanged between the pair in the hours and days after the alleged rape on 1 February at his apartment.In one message sent the following morning, Rose wrote: "Are you annoyed I didn't want to have sex," to which the man replied: "What no of course not. Was impressed by your restraint."Later that day, Rose wrote: "You violated me last night, I didn't wanna have sex."He replied: "I'm really deeply sorry, I was slightly drunk and I misread your signals, it's no excuse and I know it doesn't make it any better."In a later exchange, Rose wrote: "You basically raped me." The man replied: "That's not what I'm into at all and I feel sick."The pair later exchanged messages about money. According to the written judgement of the verdict published in March, the man agreed to reimburse Rose for her travel and accommodation. In one message, Rose wrote: "Send me the right amount you owe me for everything. Think about that. Think carefully."He transferred £5,000 that day and tried to send a further £5,000.Rose subsequently wrote: "You've given 10% of what I'm owed. 100% of my soul gone."In a later message, the man wrote: "But I don't have 100k, I have no way of paying you that."Rose reported the rape to police on 3 February. The man was arrested and questioned before being released without charge. Rose was arrested the following day and later charged with blackmail and perverting the course of public justice.

schema.headline: Isabel Rose: British woman jailed for blackmail after accusing banker of rape in Hong Kong

title: Lima: Fire kills 10 members of same family in Peru, police say
description: A fire has killed 10 members of the same family, including five children, at a home in Peru's capital Lima, police say.The blaze ripped through the pr
body: A fire has killed 10 members of the same family, including five children, at a home in Peru's capital Lima, police say.The blaze ripped through the property in the southern La Victoria district early on Wednesday, Gen Oscar Arriola from the Peruvian National Police told local media.Five children, aged between three and 15 years old, were killed, as well as four adults aged between 29 and 72, and a fifth whose age was not given. Two people were also reportedly injured. Police are investigating reports that the fire was started by arsonists. Local media say the family, who manufactured mattresses and owned electric motorcycles, had received threats and demands for extortion payments.According to a report by the government news agency Andina, external, neighbours said unknown individuals had set fire to motorcycles parked outside, with the flames spreading rapidly through flammable mattress-making materials.Seventeen members of the family were in the building when the fire started at 03:00 local time (08:00 GMT) but seven managed to escape, the Infobae news site, external reports. Listing the exact ages of the victims, the police said the children were aged three, six, six, eight and 15. The adults were aged 29, 35, 42 and 72.Arriola said their bodies were burned beyond recognition."Today, all of Peru falls silent in the face of a tragedy that plunges our nation into mourning," a statement from the National Police of Peru said.

schema.headline: Lima: Fire kills 10 members of same family in Peru, police say

title: Houthis claim attack on Saudi tankers in Red Sea
description: The Houthis named the tankers as the Encelia and the Layla, though their responsibility has not been confirmed.The group's military spokesperson Yahya
body: The Houthis named the tankers as the Encelia and the Layla, though their responsibility has not been confirmed.The group's military spokesperson Yahya Saree said they had targeted the ships with drones and missiles for allegedly violating its naval blockade. Saree added that the Houthis promised to continue their naval operations and would continue "enforcing the 'siege for a siege' equation". The state-run Saudi Press Agency confirmed early on Thursday that the Encelia was struck "while sailing in the Red Sea", resulting in a fire on its bow. Citing an unnamed official source at the Transport General Authority, it described the attack as a violation of international law. All crew were safe, it reported.UK Maritime Trade Operation (UKMTO) reported an "unknown projectile" struck a Saudi tanker about 130km from Al Shuqaiq in Saudi Arabia at 20:00 GMT. The attack caused a fire but no casualties, it said.British maritime risk management group Vanguard also reported that the Saudi tanker Encelia had been hit. The attack on the Layla has not been confirmed. US Central Command (Centcom) said at 21:30 GMT it had launched strikes at Trump's direction "to further degrade Iran's ability to threaten civilian mariners and commercial vessels transiting regional waters".The strikes targeted "Iranian military targets including maritime capabilities, missile and drone storage facilities, coastal surveillance sites, and air defense assets," it said.Kuwait, Jordan and Bahrain soon reported responding to airborne threats, before Iran's military said they had targeted US assets in all three countries, according to AFP.Iranian state media reported two people had been killed in US strikes on the country's south near its border with Iraq. Meanwhile, the Iranian Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) said the Strait of Hormuz was "completely closed" after an explosion in the channel, Iranian state media reported. The IRGC said it had stopped three oil tankers from passing through the strait.One tanker caught fire and two others turned back after attempting to pass through a mined route south of the strait, according to Iranian state media.It comes after Trump promised earlier on Wednesday to attack an Iranian bridge or power plant if its forces hit ships in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi later warned of an "eye for an eye" response to attacks on Iran.On Tuesday, Trump told reporters he would "take care of the Houthis" should they obstruct the Red Sea as they had said. Few details of the "maritime embargo" were given by the Houthis when it was announced on Monday, but a media official said they were closing the Bab al-Mandab Strait - the Red Sea's southern gateway - to Saudi vessels.The Red Sea has become crucial for oil shipments from Saudi Arabia during the US-Israeli war with Iran, which has led to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz in the Gulf.Now, the threat from the Houthis risks further disruption to international shipping, with hostilities in the region having already led to global fuel prices fluctuating significantly.So far, at least seven oil tankers have made U-turns close to Yemen since the embargo. Saree claimed the Houthis had "forced approximately ten ships to retreat and return".The disruption in the Red Sea comes as peace negotiations have effectively stalled between the US and Iran. Both countries signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) in June to halt military operations and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, as well as to reach an agreement to end the war over the following 60 days.But three weeks after it was signed, Trump declared the ceasefire "over" after Iranian attacks on ships in Hormuz, and US strikes in response.

schema.headline: Houthis claim attack on Saudi tankers in Red Sea

title: Google burning through cash with spiralling AI costs
description: Google parent Alphabet saw its business continue to grow in recent months, yet growing spending on artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure put its
body: Google parent Alphabet saw its business continue to grow in recent months, yet growing spending on artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure put its leftover cash into negative territory.The company's free cash flow, the cash it maintained after paying for operations and investments, came in at negative $5.9bn (£4.3bn) for the first time in at least a decade, according to its past financial records.Alphabet's spending on AI is now expected to hit as much as $205bn this year, an increase from $190bn, as major tech companies race to build around a new wave of the technology. Meanwhile, Alphabet's combined quarterly revenue hit $119.8bn, up 23% compared with the same time last year.But the company's stock fell 4% in after-hours trading.Anat Ashkanazi, Google's chief financial officer, noted on a call with financial analysts that the company had shown negative free cash flow due to growing capital expenditures, essentially all of which was related to AI spending.She said the company spent $45bn in the second quarter, with 60% of the cost going towards servers and the remaining 40% going towards data centres. Alphabet's capital spending was $36bn in the first quarter of this year.Ashkanazi said on the call that when it comes to AI, "the demand still outpaces that investment". "As long as we see these attractive opportunities to invest, we will continue to invest."Sundar Pichai, Google's chief executive, said that the technological shift to AI tools and capabilities still "feels like early innings in a shift across multiple areas" and that the company's plans around generating financial returns on its spending were "disciplined". "What I see with what you can do with frontier capabilities, there is still a lot of work left to do to translate that into experiences for our users. So that looks like extraordinary opportunities with extraordinary returns."Rachel Winter,  a partner at the wealth management firm Killik & Co, said there was a bit of surprise among investors about how much Google was spending."They said that this year the total they will spend will be between $195bn and $205bn. So these are huge numbers. And I think the fact that the shares dropped about 3.5% in after-hours trading when the results came out, that suggests there is a little bit of concern about those levels."Tesla, the electric vehicle company controlled by Elon Musk, also reported negative free cash flow on Wednesday of $1.1bn for the second quarter due to its own increasing investment costs.It was the company's first negative showing of leftover cash in two years, according to its financial records.Vaibhav Taneja, Tesla's chief financial officer, said during a call with analysts that the company will spend as much as $25bn this year, more than double its capital spending in 2025.He added that Tesla was in "a big investment cycle" and that its spending would probably increase further over the next three years.Tesla's stock also dropped by 4% in after-hours trading.

schema.headline: Google burning through cash with spiralling AI costs

title: Wildberries: Russia's businesses under strain from Ukraine's attacks on retail warehouses
description: Ukrainian drones have hit several retail warehouses belonging to Russia's biggest online retailer, Wildberries, in a matter of days, dealing a blow to
body: Ukrainian drones have hit several retail warehouses belonging to Russia's biggest online retailer, Wildberries, in a matter of days, dealing a blow to a large number of small and medium-sized businesses.After months of drone attacks on oil depots and refineries, Kyiv says it is now targeting logistics hubs used by Russians to buy military equipment, and disrupting supplies to the Russian army.But it has also frustrated hundreds of Russian sellers who depend on online marketplaces for their livelihoods, making the war feel closer to home after almost four and a half years."I am a mother of a disabled child, I've built the business myself," one woman complained on an expletive-laden social media post that went viral."I've lost 1m roubles (£9,500; $12,700)... We're already getting screwed over from every direction as it is."In Ukraine's first attack on Wildberries at the weekend, two warehouses were hit, at Elektrostal east of Moscow and in Kotovsk in the Tambov region, some 400km (250 miles) from the Ukrainian border.Seven workers on a nightshift were killed in Kotovsk and one was fatally wounded at Elektrostal. Dozens more were injured and goods worth an estimated at £750m ($1bn) were destroyed.Kyiv said it had struck "major logistics facilities" used to "supply sanctioned components for drone production and navigation equipment".Unlike Western retail giant Amazon, which sells goods directly alongside its marketplace business, Wildberries and Ozon act almost entirely as marketplaces, connecting independent sellers with buyers across Russia, storing and delivering their goods and handling payments.This means many of the losses incurred in the attacks have been borne by businesses.For one woman, who sells stationery items, Wildberries is the only distribution channel. The day after she delivered her goods to the Elektrostal warehouse near Moscow last Friday, she learned that it had all gone up in flames.She still has other inventory left, but this time of the year is important for her business, as parents and students stock up for the new autumn term.Another Wildberries trader who sells teenage clothing, told the BBC she had lost about 1.4m roubles (£13,300) worth of stock. She is halting new supplies for a few weeks."The outlook for working with Wildberries is very murky," she said. "Right now I have enough of a safety cushion to survive the current losses, but if there are more strikes, I probably won't manage."Neither woman expects compensation. Earlier this month, less than two weeks before the attacks, a clause was added to their contracts, including drone attacks in the list of "force majeure events" for which Wildberries is not liable.Company owner Tatiana Kim has promised "not to abandon" the sellers and some compensation has been paid to a number of small businesses. She has also offered temporary discounts and waived shipment fees, but this has done little to calm fears of further attacks.Supporting small and medium-sized businesses has been among Vladimir Putin's declared priorities even in wartime, and framed as a backbone of resilience in the face of Western sanctions.But the Russian state has been unable to shield the sector from the war's impact, and there have been several shocks to businesses since the start of the year.VAT went up from 20% to 22% in January, with extra funding earmarked for defence, and tax breaks for some businesses were scrapped.Some 209,000 small and medium-sized companies closed in the first quarter of 2026, according to business intelligence platform Kontur.Fokus. That is 9% more than in the first three months of 2025.Widespread internet shutdowns and a crackdown on popular messaging apps led to further troubles, and Moscow businesses lost tens of millions of dollars in one week in March, according to some estimates.And then came the fuel crisis, triggered by Ukrainian strikes on oil depots, refineries and supply routes. "There are already a lot of nails, and they keep hammering them in. Is [the attack on Wildberries warehouses] another nail in the coffin? Well, yes, of course," Prof Ruben Eniklopov of Pompeu Fabra University in Barcelona told the BBC.Small businesses in Russia have proved resilient in the past, but inflation is high, the budget deficit is widening, and oil and gas revenues are 23% lower than in the first six months of last year.Russia has vast financial reserves, accumulated in peacetime, but its war in Ukraine is increasingly swallowing resources, starving the civilian economy of growth.Additional reporting by Olga Shamina.

schema.headline: Wildberries: Russia's businesses under strain from Ukraine's attacks on retail warehouses

title: China's animal lovers take fight against abuse offline and overseas
description: When videos showing the horrific killing of a stray dog and her puppies sparked outrage on the Chinese internet in June, Beijing's censorship machine
body: When videos showing the horrific killing of a stray dog and her puppies sparked outrage on the Chinese internet in June, Beijing's censorship machine kicked into action.But what was unusual this time was that it pitted the shocked and angry voices against authorities who said they were concerned about the four boys involved, all of them younger than 14.China's Communist Party regime tightly controls media and the internet to suppress criticism of the state and to manage public anger. But in this case authorities cited the age of the boys as they urged people to withhold their comments and scrubbed social media posts about the incident. "We urge the public not to disseminate related information, to reject cyberbullying, and to work together to create a good environment for the growth of minors," reads an official statement.But the restrictions only seemed to spur the outcry. China's animal lovers have now taken their fight offline and abroad, even putting up billboards in New York to drum up support.In China, posts about the incident are still drawing thousands of comments across social media, three weeks after the incident. There is shock, anger and sorrow, as well as demands for laws against animal cruelty, which China currently does not have.Disturbing footage showing the boys in the city of Jieyang, Guangdong province, hitting three puppies with sticks before setting their mother on fire emerged online at the end of June. The boys were seen laughing as the dog, named Wang Wang, burned. Jieyang authorities said last month that the boys would not face criminal charges and would instead be sent to a "specialised school", but did not elaborate.Some reports suggest the boys filmed themselves and uploaded the video online, but police have not commented on that. Censors have tried to block the video to protect the boys' identity, although some people argue that it should stay. Their identities have not been revealed, but snippets of the video with their faces visible continue to circulate online. There are also unverified posts claiming to be close-ups of the boys' faces, raising the risk of other children being wrongly identified. Countries around the world restrict information about child offenders or set a high bar for identifying them. For instance, media coverage and public access to juvenile court documents are often restricted to minimise psychological harm caused by undue publicity.In this case, Chinese authorities have not completely banned the discussion yet. Keywords such as "Wang Wang" and "animal protection" came up as trending search terms on Monday, but posts related to them started to disappear towards the end of this week. Some users have also reported that their ability to share others' posts has been restricted. It is hard to say if the deleted or restricted content had named or identified any of the four boys. Officials have not said anything more on the matter. Posts by several celebrities, including Chinese actor Du Jiang and Taiwanese actress Joe Chen, who had called for China to strengthen protection for animals also disappeared. But it's unclear if these were taken down by the celebrities themselves.Chinese users have refused to back down though, with some turning to advocates abroad to air Wang Wang's plight. It is hard to say why this, of all the sensitive issues that are censored in China, has galvanised such pushback.Part of the reason could be that it's the latest in a string of animal abuse cases that have made headlines recently. And the footage seems to have had a visceral impact. Animal lover PL tells BBC Chinese she found it "affecting in a particularly severe way". "We can't make our voices heard, so many people [outside China] are actively helping us to share this message," the woman in eastern China says.The International Organisation for Animal Protection based in Italy says it has received more than 100 reports from people across the world, calling for the organisation to intervene. It added that it had sought more information from Chinese authorities.In New York City's Times Square, one of the busiest pedestrian intersections in the world, photographs of Wang Wang come up on a gigantic digital screen every 35 minutes.One of the captions reads: "She was loved. She wanted to live. She should still be here." Similar billboards have been put up on a building near Taipei 101, an iconic 101-floor skyscraper in Taiwan. This one includes a picture of Wang Wang nursing her puppies. "This planet is their home too. Justice for Wang Wang," the caption reads.CK, a Taiwanese woman who spent NT$30,000 ($930; £692) putting up the billboard, says she hopes it can foster "a sense of unity"."It makes everyone feel like we're speaking out together on this issue, rather than airing our individual opinions online," she tells BBC Chinese. CK has also set up accounts on platforms like Reddit and X to share the animals' plight."The world need to know about the cruelty these dogs have been subject to," CK says, adding that she couldn't sleep or eat properly for days after seeing a clip of the brutal killing.Elara, another Taiwanese woman, used AI to translate a summary of the incident into more than 10 languages, such as Japanese, Portuguese and Russian, which have been circulated online.Residents in Hong Kong and Macau have raised funds to put up posters on buses and trains. Within mainland China, the debate continues, with much of the conversation centred around creating awareness to demand laws against animal cruelty.   A striking cartoon of Wang Wang and her puppies caught the eye of many football fans during a domestic game at a stadium in Dalian on Saturday. "Every life deserves respect and kindness," read the cartoon that was displayed on a huge screen before the game.Homegrown businesses, such as beauty brand Amortals and streetwear label Wah Yan Ching Nin, have also placed ads outside shopping malls across the country, calling for an end to animal abuse.Stickers and car decals featuring Wang Wang and her puppies have also been put up for sale on China's largest e-commerce platform Taobao. Some of these efforts have met roadblocks. Wan Jia Li, a mall in the city of Changsha, was reportedly instructed to remove billboards featuring Wang Wang. "We can't do animal-protection billboards anymore... My boss told me so. I'm not sure if the notice came from the government, or why we have been asked to take them down," an employee in the mall's management team told the BBC.Still, people have continued to rally online."Let's keep posting. Don't let the heat of discussion cool down," reads a comment on Weibo."Constantly speaking up does not mean disturbing social order. [We're doing this] to let more people see it and realise it's such an important thing that we do not mistreat animals," reads another.

schema.headline: China's animal lovers take fight against abuse offline and overseas

title: US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia
description: The US and Saudi Arabia have signed a landmark deal that will allow the Gulf kingdom to develop a civilian nuclear programme.The deal is described as
body: The US and Saudi Arabia have signed a landmark deal that will allow the Gulf kingdom to develop a civilian nuclear programme.The deal is described as a "peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement" that will provide "great access for American companies in the Saudi nuclear energy program" by the US energy department.Text from the deal has not been made public, but US media report that it could allow Saudi Arabia to enrich uranium in the future - which can be used as fuel for power plants, but also to make nuclear bombs.Experts say that would mark a first for the US and could run the risk of future nuclear proliferation in an already unstable region.The US Department of Energy said a "bilateral safeguards agreement" had been signed along with the co-operation deal. "Together, these two agreements lay the legal foundation for a decades-long, multi-billion-dollar partnership that advances several priority economic and strategic objectives, including nuclear nonproliferation," the statement said. The Saudi government also released a statement saying the deal "represents an extension of existing co-operation between the two countries in the energy sector", noting that it comes after the Saudi leader visited Washington last November.The nuclear issue has been at the centre of the months-long US-Israeli conflict with Iran. The two nations say one of the reasons they attacked Iran in February was to prevent the country from ever developing a nuclear weapon - something that Tehran denies planning to do.Saudi Arabia's de facto ruler, Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, told CBS News in 2018 that his own nation was not planning to acquire a nuclear weapon, but that "without a doubt if Iran developed a nuclear bomb, we will follow suit as soon as possible".Wednesday's announcement came as fighting escalates between the US and Iran. Saudi Arabia, a longtime US ally, hosts several US bases that have been targeted by Iranian attacks. It also comes two days after Iran's allies in Yemen, the Houthis, announced a "maritime blockade" against the kingdom. Specific details of the nuclear deal have not been given but earlier media reports , externalsuggested that, after undertaking a joint study with the US, Saudi Arabia may be allowed to enrich uranium on its soil to produce fuel at a US-built enrichment facility, instead of relying on imported fuel.This could be worth billions of dollars to the US, whose companies could be involved in building a facility to produce enriched uranium, which can be used as fuel for power plants but also to make nuclear bombs."Rest assured, these agreements uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation, while relying on the world's best nuclear technology and scientists, designed right here in the United States," US Secretary of Energy Chris Wright said. The energy department said the deal would advance both "US and regional security", and would strengthen America's "competitive edge in civil nuclear technology".Nuclear experts and former officials in Israel - the US's top ally in the Middle East - have voiced concern that the plan could ultimately lead to Saudi Arabia being able to develop a nuclear weapon - though other experts say that path would likely take decades and spark pushback from the international community. Israeli Economy Minister Nir Barkat said his country "can manage it" if Saudi Arabia had nuclear power for peaceful means.Israel itself is widely believed to have nuclear weapons, but it neither confirms nor denies that this is the case.Rosemary Kelanic, the director of the Middle East programme at the US-based Defense Priorities think tank, said it would be "quite shocking" if the US were to allow Saudi Arabia uranium enrichment facilities."The US has never done that before. We have never helped another country enrich on their own soil," she said, adding that this was because "if you can produce your own nuclear fuel, you become a much bigger risk" for building nuclear weapons.The agreement will now be sent to the US Congress for review.  Some lawmakers on both sides of the political aisle are expected to oppose the deal, though Trump's Republican Party controls both the upper and lower chambers and opponents of the deal do not appear to have the votes needed to block it.US Secretary of State Marco Rubio rebuffed concerns that it could advance Saudi Arabia's ability to develop nuclear arms."The US is not going to reach any agreement with any country in the world that leads to the risk of proliferation," Rubio told reporters.Several Democrats opposed to the deal pointed out that, during his time as a senator, Rubio had endorsed a law - the "No Nuclear Weapons for Saudi Arabia Act" - which required Congressional approval of any nuclear deal. Massachusetts Democratic Senator Edward Markey condemned Rubio and Trump as "allowing Saudi Arabia, a belligerent and authoritarian nation, to develop nuclear weapons technologies while starting a war with Iran under the guise of preventing an Iranian nuclear bomb", adding that their "hypocrisy is matched only by their recklessness".If an enrichment provision is included, it would distinguish this agreement from another one struck by the US with the United Arab Emirates in 2009, under which the UAE does not produce its own fuel and accepts a host of other restrictions.For that reason, the agreement is also expected to attract concern from nuclear non-proliferation campaigners, whose ultimate aim is to prevent an increase in the number of nuclear weapons worldwide.But energy sector expert Bob McNally, a former member of US President George W Bush's National Security Council, suggested the US was not creating a "great" security risk. "I think we have a good record of working with countries to responsibly develop their nuclear sectors," he told the BBC.Saudi Arabia has been lobbying for such a deal for years, but Washington had held out on the proviso that it recognises Israel, a key US ally, and normalises diplomatic and economic relations with it. But that demand appears to have now been dropped, according to US media reports. Trump has worked to cultivate a closer relationship with Saudi Arabia during his time in the White House. Along with the strategic and commercial gains likely to come with this deal, one possible reason for this agreement happening now is that Russia and China have reportedly been in discussions to strike a similar deal and the US might not have wanted to miss out.The deal marks a major victory for Saudi Arabia, and it has the potential to reshape power dynamics in the Middle East - though not in the immediate future.

schema.headline: US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia

title: Russians in Angola handed down jail terms for spying and terrorism
description: Two Russians have been found guilty of terrorism and spying by a court in Angola's capital, Luanda.Political consultant Igor Ratchin was sentenced to
body: Two Russians have been found guilty of terrorism and spying by a court in Angola's capital, Luanda.Political consultant Igor Ratchin was sentenced to 11 years in prison and translator Lev Lakshtanov was given eight years. They had denied the charges.They were arrested last August over accusations of stirring up anti-government protests, conducting a campaign of disinformation and attempting to interfere in next year's presidential election. The Russians' lawyers had previously requested their extradition to Russia under bilateral co-operation agreements. The BBC has not been able to establish whether the court approved that request.In the indictment, seen by the BBC, the authorities alleged that Ratchin and Lakshtanov were involved in a political influence operation linked to Africa Politology, which the Angolan authorities described as a Russian political network that emerged from the now-defunct Wagner mercenary group.Prosecutors further alleged Africa Politology sought to spread anti-Western narratives, cultivate political contacts and influence politics in Angola ahead of next year's presidential elections.The defendants had denied any connection to Wagner or the Russian state, saying they were working on plans to establish a Russian cultural centre in Luanda.Political operatives linked to Wagner have been active across Africa for more than a decade, in particular in the Central African Republic, Mali and Madagascar.Two Angolans, sports journalist Amor Carlos Tomé and political activist Francisco Oliveira, were also on trial on similar charges for their alleged part in the Russian-linked influence operation, which they both denied.Tomé received a two-year suspended sentence and Oliveira was acquitted.Prosecutors appealed immediately after the judgement, which was read out after 23:00 local time on Tuesday.Tomé is expected to remain in custody pending the outcome of the appeal.The BBC has contacted the Russian embassy in Angola for comment but had not received a response at the time of publication.Elizeu Nguiniti, the lawyer representing Ratchin and Lakshtanov, told the BBC he would not comment on the decision.After the judgement was read out, he told the court he disagreed with the verdict, which found his clients guilty of four of 11 charges - criminal association, espionage, terrorism and illegal retention of currency - but said he accepted the court's decision.According to Angola's privately owned news site Novo Jornal, the Russians told the court during previous hearings they had held private meetings with political figures from both the ruling MPLA and opposition Unita parties, but denied that the meetings were connected to any illegal activity.It reported that the court had rejected a defence request to call several of those politicians as witnesses, ruling that their testimony was "not necessary" for establishing the facts of the case.Prosecutors alleged that the defendants played a role in organising deadly unrest in Angola in 2025. They alleged the Russians paid local journalists and experts more than $24,000 (£18,000) to spread pro-Russian and anti-government content aimed at provoking political change in Angola.They also cited publications linked to the operation, notes and photographs related to the 2025 protests found on the defendants' devices, as well as alleged contacts with senior Angolan political figures.Many Angolan activists strongly disagree with the prosecution's argument that the protests did not happen organically.Novo Jornal previously reported that Lakshtanov told the court during one of the hearings that he had lived and worked in Angola for nearly four decades and that his long academic career was incompatible with participation in terrorist organisations.Earlier Ratchin had explained to the court that he was a businessman who had come to Angola to establish a Russian cultural centre and that payments he made to various individuals were compensation for services rendered, Novo Jornal reported.He reportedly said he was surprised by his arrest in August 2025 and the subsequent charges.Early proceedings of the trial, which began in March, were marked by a controversy.During one of the initial hearings, the prosecutor declined to read the indictment aloud in court. She argued that the defendants were already aware of the accusations against them and therefore formal reading was not required.The defence disagreed. They argued that since the trial was public, those attending the proceedings, including journalists, should be able to hear and understand the allegations.Nevertheless, the prosecutor maintained that reading the indictment was optional and refused to do so.The indictment itself had been criticised even before the trial began.The defence has since consistently challenged its contents, arguing it often relied on assumptions rather than concrete evidence.The BBC had previously identified a number of misspellings and apparent factual errors in the document, but received no clarification from the prosecution about these.Additional reporting by Ilya Barabanov and BBC Russian's Investigations Team

schema.headline: Russians in Angola handed down jail terms for spying and terrorism

title: Jantar Mantar CJP protests in Delhi: The meme language of India's Gen Z
description: Indian police cracked down on 'cockroach' protesters. They went home and made memes about itImage source, Getty ImagesByZoya MateenCorrespondentReport
body: Indian police cracked down on 'cockroach' protesters. They went home and made memes about itImage source, Getty ImagesByZoya MateenCorrespondentReporting fromDelhiOn Monday, 20-year-old Chhavi (name changed) paused outside a Delhi metro station on her way to what would become one of the largest anti-government demonstrations against Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government in the Indian capital in recent years.She balanced her phone against a railing, stepped back and filmed a quick "fit check" - a video showing off her outfit before the protest."Going to get beaten by cops, guys," she smiled into the camera.Hours later, police fired tear gas and charged demonstrators with batons as tens of thousands of supporters of the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) – a youth-led movement that grew from protests over exam irregularities into a broader campaign for government accountability – marched towards parliament. They demanded the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan over exam paper leaks and recruitment scandals. Pradhan has refused to resign, accusing the opposition of exploiting students for political gain. Authorities suspended mobile internet around the protest site. By evening, protesters said scores had been beaten by police, while police said several officers had also been injured.When Chhavi finally uploaded the videos that evening, one thing stood out. Before the march, she joked about being beaten by police. Hours later, she was still smiling - posing beside riot officers, flashing victory signs, filming through crowds and even interviewing an injured protester. The clips were among thousands of reels, memes and first-person dispatches that flooded Instagram once protesters regained internet access. Some were raw with anger. Others were breathless, frightened or quietly defiant. Yet it was often the sardonic humour that drew the eye.Image source, Deeva AroraImage caption, A lot of protesters posted "fit checks" - a viral Instagram trend - ahead of the marchOne young woman calmly reapplied her lipstick in front of a line of riot police. Another joked she was "serving face at the protest because the government couldn't serve justice". One reel began as a routine outfit reveal before abruptly cutting to protesters fleeing tear gas. Another gave a deadpan "review" of tear gas, rating its smell and sting like a new restaurant.To many watching online, the humour seemed strangely at odds with the violence. For the young people making the videos, there was no contradiction.None of the protesters interviewed for this story spoke about algorithms, engagement or going viral. They described filming as almost inseparable from protesting itself.And when they turned to humour, it was almost always self-directed: joking about the kilometres logged fleeing baton charges or the absurdity of filming an outfit reveal moments before running into tear gas. The jokes did not erase the fear. They coexisted with it.Experts say the trend hints at a broader shift in how a generation raised online experiences protest."Older generations separated political life from everyday life," said Joyojeet Pal, a professor at the University of Michigan who studies technology and society."Younger generations grow up with politics alongside their memes, their family pictures and their daily commutes." A protest, in other words, becomes just another moment to record.Chhavi continued to film even after the crackdown began. Some commenters later accused her of treating the violence as content.Image source, ReutersImage caption, From one-liners to satirical digs, the placards have become one of the defining features of the protests"But I was adrenaline-soaked, scared, petrified," she said. "It was either laugh in the face of fear or cry."That instinct to keep filming, even as the situation turned violent, is one that Payal Arora, a digital anthropologist at Utrecht University, says has become increasingly common around the world. Smartphones have turned ordinary people into chroniclers of political upheaval, recording moments that might otherwise disappear."The act of capturing a protest is often an end in itself," she said. "Their message is more like: 'This happened. We were there. We will remember.'"The humour, too, followed a pattern. No young protester described the day as funny. They spoke instead of tear gas, baton charges and the bewilderment of watching a peaceful march descend into chaos. The jokes came later, almost instinctively, as a way of carrying that experience.People often mistake that humour for detachment, says Nishant Shah, director of Digital Narratives Center at  School of Journalism & Communication at the Chinese University of Hong Kong. In reality, he argues, it serves almost the opposite purpose."Politics is everyday, emotional and experiential," he said.Humour has long been part of protest movements. It can create solidarity, ease tension and help people endure moments of fear."Even when the cause is brutal and the fight fierce," Shah said, "there is joy, laughter, songs and hope. Without those things, protests don't sustain themselves."Ishaan (name changed), 21, came to Delhi for his first protest. When tear gas shells landed nearby, he briefly considered leaving. Instead, he reached for his phone.Image source, EPA/ShutterstockImage caption, Police fired tear gas to disperse protesters as they tried to reach parliamentThe reel he posted later showed white smoke drifting across the street beneath a simple caption: "Delhi Police doesn't realise I'm from Jamuna Paar."For most Delhiites, the joke needed little explanation. Jamuna Paar - literally "across the Yamuna" - is shorthand for the city's eastern neighbourhoods and the subject of endless teasing over their neglected civic infrastructure."The smell there is worse than the tear gas," Ishan said, laughing.He told the BBC the joke was never about pretending nothing had happened."It was about refusing to let fear dictate the memory of the day," he said.Protests have always found ways to hold fear and hope in the same frame.India's freedom movement had songs and speeches. Trade unions marched behind banners. Student movements embraced posters and street theatre. More recently, the anti-citizenship protests were marked by murals and public readings of the Constitution, while the farmers' movement became synonymous with tractor rallies and livestreams from Delhi's borders.Like many CJP protesters, Ishan spoke in the idiom of a generation raised online. To dismiss that language as frivolous, Shah argues, is to misunderstand what it is doing."We often make the mistake of trying to understand new technologies through older ways of thinking," Shah said.Image source, ManitImage caption, Manit even logged a running activity while evading a police baton chargeBut a meme is not simply today's version of a political poster, he said. It gathers meaning as people remake it, parody it and pass it on. Sharing it becomes another way of participating in the protest.That was true for Manit, an 18-year-old engineering student. It was his first protest, and he had almost decided not to film."I was scared," he said. Then he looked around. "Everyone was recording, talking to the camera, just being themselves. So I asked my friend to record me."At first glance, the reel resembled the workout videos he usually posted. Only the final frame revealed the distance on his fitness app had been logged while fleeing police baton charges."I escaped the police," he laughed. "I ran and ran and ended up completing my workout too."For Manit, that irony was precisely the point."Not all young people read the news," he said. "If humour can make important conversations accessible, why wouldn't we use it?"*Some names have been changed to protect the identity of the personFollow BBC News India on Instagram, external, YouTube,, external X, external and Facebook, external.

schema.headline: Jantar Mantar CJP protests in Delhi: The meme language of India's Gen Z

title: Karen Carter: Police formally investigate woman after Brit stabbed to death in French village
description: A woman has been placed under formal investigation over the death last year in France of a British-South African mother-of-four. Karen Carter, 65, was
body: A woman has been placed under formal investigation over the death last year in France of a British-South African mother-of-four. Karen Carter, 65, was found lying near her car with stab wounds by a friend in Trémolat, the Dordogne village where she had lived for over a decade and managed two holiday rental homes.Prosecutors say the 70-year-old woman was originally taken into custody in 2025 as part of preliminary inquiries before being released.Jacques-Edouard Andrault, the investigating judge in the case, said in a statement on Wednesday that "the investigations conducted as part of the judicial inquiry opened on 7 May, 2025, have led the investigating judge to formally charge her".Being placed under formal investigation in French law means an investigating judge has to decide whether or not there is enough evidence for the case to go to trial.At the time of the killing in April last year, Karen Carter's husband, Alan, said that he first heard of her death through a cousin who had read about it on a local community Facebook page.He said he received confirmation of the death after his assistant phoned local police.Describing his wife as an outgoing, friendly person who "wouldn't hurt a fly", he said her death has been "traumatic" for his family."I'm an introvert, and she's the exact opposite. She's an extrovert, she loves people, she loves to have fun. People love her, she has a good heart," he said."She's the one who would bring home the lost dog, or cat, or whatever. She's that sort of person. Everyone liked her. That's why I married her. She's just lovely."

schema.headline: Karen Carter: Police formally investigate woman after Brit stabbed to death in French village

title: CJP, Jantar Mantar: Pressure grows on Indian PM Modi's government as 'cockroach' protests intensify
description: On Monday, tens of thousands of protesters flooded central Delhi after the CJP called for a march to parliament as the monsoon session began. At least
body: On Monday, tens of thousands of protesters flooded central Delhi after the CJP called for a march to parliament as the monsoon session began. At least 60 people were injured after police used batons and tear gas to disperse the crowds. Officers also dismantled the stage and makeshift tents at Jantar Mantar, the protest's main base near the 300-year-old observatory. But the demonstrators have refused to leave, maintaining a round-the-clock sit-in.A day after the violence, they were visited by several senior opposition leaders such as former Delhi chief minister Arvind Kejriwal and veteran politician Sharad Pawar.Later on Tuesday, Congress president Mallikarjun Kharge and Gandhi led a surprise march to Modi's house, demanding the resignations of Pradhan, Home Minister Amit Shah who the Delhi police report to and PM Modi. Why are thousands protesting for education reform in India?The hunger-striking engineer demanding education reforms in IndiaGandhi said the government must apologise to students for the crackdown, adding  that the Congress decided to stage a protest outside Modi's house because they weren't allowed to raise the issue in the parliament.He said he had met speaker Om Birla to seek a discussion, but "the speaker said he needed to take permission from the government, but it was very clear to us that there was no interest in the government of having a debate". Federal Minister Jitendra Singh denied Gandhi's allegation, external - he said he informed the Congress MP that the government had accepted his demand for a discussion but alleged that Gandhi later also demanded the resignation of Pradhan.He accused Gandhi of "changing the goalpost" and of refusing to end the protest despite being told that the place wasn't conducive to such demonstrations.Later, Pradhan - in his first comments since Monday's violence - criticised the Congress for "shamelessly exploiting students as political tools"."Even after the government conveyed its readiness for a comprehensive discussion, the Congress chose political spectacle over democratic debate. Their objective was never solutions for students, it was disruption for political headlines," he said in a post on X.Pradhan did not directly refer to the CJP or the protests but said: "We owe our students more than outrage. We owe them answers, reforms and accountability. That is exactly what our government remains committed to delivering."Pradhan's resignation has been one of the key demands of the protesters and the CJP has not responded to his comments so far.A satirical political movement, CJP was inspired by remarks made earlier this year by India's chief justice, who used the word "cockroach" to describe unemployed young people turning to journalism and activism. After the comments sparked outrage, he said he had been referring only to those with "fake and bogus degrees".Monday's huge turnout showed that the CJP, though not a registered political party, has evolved into a movement capable of drawing tens of thousands onto the streets.Protesters, who have been camped for the past month at Jantar Mantar, gained global attention after activist and educationist Sonam Wangchuk joined them on 28 June and began an indefinite hunger strike in support of their demands.He was forcibly removed from the protest site by the police on Saturday and taken to hospital where he continues to fast.The movement has broadened into one of the most visible expressions of public dissent against Modi in recent years.Over the past few days, support for the CJP has grown beyond Delhi, with protests also being held in other major cities like Mumbai, Hyderabad, Bengaluru and Ahmedabad.

schema.headline: CJP, Jantar Mantar: Pressure grows on Indian PM Modi's government as 'cockroach' protests intensify

title: Tankers make sharp U-turns after Houthi shipping threat
description: ByThomas Copeland and Joshua CheethamBBC VerifyAt least seven oil tankers have made U-turns close to Yemen since the Iran-linked Houthi group announce
body: ByThomas Copeland and Joshua CheethamBBC VerifyAt least seven oil tankers have made U-turns close to Yemen since the Iran-linked Houthi group announced a "maritime embargo" against Saudi Arabia on Monday, ship-tracking data shows.All of the ships were travelling either to or from Saudi ports and made sharp changes of course after the embargo was announced.Nearly 15% of global sea trade moves through the Red Sea, which connects the Mediterranean and Gulf of Aden through two choke points - the Suez Canal in Egypt and the Bab al-Mandab Strait between Yemen and the Horn of Africa.The Red Sea has been a key route for exports of Saudi oil since the US-Israeli war with Iran led to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which has severely restricted the flow of oil and gas from the Gulf.This has played a "major role in relieving pressure to global markets" and if the Houthis further restrict trade then "prices will go up", said Rosemary Kelanic from the US-based Defense Priorities think tank.The day before the Houthis announced their blockade, 20 vessels entered the Red Sea and 22 exited via the Bab al-Mandeb strait, according to maritime intelligence firm Kpler.Kelanic told BBC Verify that any Houthi attacks on vessels in the Red Sea or Gulf of Aden "will suppress all international traffic not just Saudi-bound vessels".The EU's naval force in the region Aspides recommended on Wednesday that "merchant vessels linked to Israeli, US or Saudi interests avoid transiting the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden until the threat level decreases", according to an advisory seen by Reuters news agency.Ship-tracking data from MarineTraffic shows the crude oil tanker Rodos, loaded with Saudi crude, departed for India from the Red Sea port of al-Muajiz on Monday evening. The Liberian-flagged ship set off south towards the Bab al-Mandab Strait but about nine hours later made a U-turn and began sailing north instead.The Liberian-flagged oil and chemical tanker Mica departed Saudi Arabia's Jazan port on Monday afternoon and also headed south before reversing course.Ship-tracking data shows at least eight ships bound for the Red Sea have made U-turns instead of entering the Bab al-Mandab Strait from the south as well.Marshall Islands-flagged vehicle carrier Liu Jiang Kou was sailing to Saudi Arabia's Jeddah port from China before completely changing course on Monday in the Gulf of Aden.A Hong Kong-flagged crude oil tanker sailing from China called New Prime also turned around as it headed towards Saudi Arabia on Monday and now appears to be waiting in the Arabian Sea.While the reasons for each course change cannot be independently verified, "the timing is consistent with the embargo announcement," said maritime risk management company Vanguard.In an email to shipowners on Monday, which the BBC has seen, the Houthis said "vessels are banned from loading or discharging cargo at any Saudi ports". It added that ships "may be subject to targeting in any location within the operational reach".Image source, EPAImage caption, Houthi fighters during a protest against Saudi Arabia in Yemen's capital Sana'a on 17 JulyThe Houthis have said their embargo is in retaliation to a Saudi blockade of ports and airports in Houthi-controlled north-western Yemen.Saudi Arabia has condemned the allegation and has vowed to "take all necessary measures to protect its ships in accordance with international law".Yemen has been devastated by a civil war since 2014, when the Houthis captured the capital of Sana'a from the government. The conflict escalated in 2015, after a Saudi-led coalition of Arab states intervened to try and restore the official government.There has been an informal truce between the Houthis and Saudi Arabia since 2022. But last week, the Houthis launched missiles at an airport in south-western Saudi Arabia in response to air strikes on Sanaa's airport that they blamed on the kingdom.The Houthis have previously caused severe disruption to the the 32km-wide (20-mile) Bab al-Mandab Strait.Shortly after the Gaza war broke out in October 2023, the Houthis began attacking merchant vessels in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden, saying they were acting in support of the Palestinians. These attacks led to four ships being sunk, one vessel seized, and nine crew members killed.Since the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, Saudi Arabia has diverted more than 70% of its crude exports from the Gulf to the Red Sea port of Yanbu through an east-west pipeline.About four million barrels per day have been shipped from Yanbu in recent weeks, compared with about 973,000 a year earlier, according to data from Kpler and maritime analytics platform Signal Ocean.Naveen Das, a senior oil analyst from Kpler, told BBC Verify the only option left to the Saudis if the Bab al-Mandab Strait is effectively closed would be to move oil all the way through the Mediterranean and around the southern tip of Africa to get to Asia.The impact of the blockade on consumers won't be immediate, but the conflict with Iran has made the global energy system more vulnerable to shocks like these, Das added. "We could weather it for two weeks, three weeks, even a month, but even in that time, we will see higher freight rates, higher energy prices and that bleeds into higher energy consumer costs."Ships close to the Red Sea are also broadcasting messages in an attempt to deter attacks, a common practise in the region since the Houthis targeted vessels during the war in Gaza.At least 50 vessels in the region are broadcasting they have armed guards on board, according to data from MarineTraffic.Martin Kelly, from crisis management firm EOS Risk Group, told BBC Verify that captains will fear the "Houthis might read your situation wrong" and think their ships have "called at a Saudi port in the past, then therefore you could be attacked"."A lot of these ships are very large crude oil carriers. I think the damage would be catastrophic. There would be certainly be deaths onboard these ships," he added.Additional reporting by Barbara Metzler

schema.headline: Tankers make sharp U-turns after Houthi shipping threat

title: Austrian police station opens at Hitler's birthplace in bid to rid site of Nazi link
description: Austria has opened a police station in the house where Adolf Hitler was born in the town of Braunau am Inn.It follows years of controversy about what
body: Austria has opened a police station in the house where Adolf Hitler was born in the town of Braunau am Inn.It follows years of controversy about what to do with the 17th-Century former inn, where Nazi dictator Adolf Hitler was born in April 1889.The building has now been substantially refurbished and its facade changed, but it has been a huge headache for Austrian authorities, who want to prevent it from becoming a site of neo-Nazi pilgrimage.Hitler only lived in Salzburger Vorstadt 15 for a few weeks before his family moved to another address in Braunau. They left the town for good when he was three years old.Hitler returned briefly to Braunau in 1938, on his way to Vienna, after he annexed Austria to Nazi Germany.The opening ceremony for the police station, featuring police musicians playing a tuba and other brass instruments, was attended by Interior Minister Gerhard Karner and other local dignitaries.Austria was taking "responsibility" for the "terrible legacy" of the past, said Karner. While deciding what to do with the building had not been easy, Karner believed the police station marked "a new chapter" for it."This house will now become a place of democracy, rule of law, security, freedom and human dignity," he said.A Catholic police chaplain then blessed two crosses which will hang in the new police station.For decades, the interior ministry had rented the building from its former owner, a local woman called Gerlinde Pommer. It paid her a generous rent, in an attempt to prevent the house being used as a site for extreme right-wing tourism.The interior minister at the time, Wolfgang Sobotka, said the building should be torn down and a new one erected in its place. It was an echo of an earlier offer by a Russian MP who said he wanted to buy the house and blow it up.But there was a storm of criticism, with many saying that destroying the house would be tantamount to denying Austria's Nazi past.In 2019, the government announced plans to turn it into a police station and initiated an EU-wide competition for architects to submit designs.Millions of euros were spent refurbishing the old yellow building, which now looks very different from before. Now painted white, inside and out, the building is full of gleaming oak woodwork. An old winding staircase and arched entrance hall have been preserved.It looks very different from when the Lebenshilfe charity was running a small shop in one of the rooms at the front, when I last went inside almost 20 years ago.Police Chief Inspector Ludwig Heise said he was happy to have such "a great, modern building" to work in. "We have more space," he said, "but otherwise it is like any other police station in Austria."Not everyone is happy with the decision to open a police station there, with some arguing it sends the wrong signal. Some had wanted the house to become a centre of responsibility, confronting the Nazi past. Others, like local historian Florian Kotanko, say they would have liked to have seen the Lebenshilfe charity return."But even this charity organisation did not want to come back because they thought they would be used as a fig leaf for a political discussion, and so the government had to decide in another way," Kotanko told the BBC.There is no sign on the house about its history and the building features security cameras to record the activities of any potential neo-Nazi pilgrims.All that links it to Hitler is a stone on the street outside, which was erected in 1989.It says: "Never again Fascism; In memory of millions of dead." The stone comes from the nearby former concentration camp at Mauthausen. Hitler's name does not appear. However, Florian Kotanko believes the site's history will never go away even if fewer neo-Nazis pay a visit."You have to deal with it, and so we all try to deal with it in an open and correct way. It's not a matter of being proud, but it's a matter of dealing with the fact."

schema.headline: Austrian police station opens at Hitler's birthplace in bid to rid site of Nazi link

title: Mamdani backs off  pledge to arrest Netanyahu citing lack of authority
description: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani, who previously pledged to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu if he visited the city, now says he does not have the "authori
body: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani, who previously pledged to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu if he visited the city, now says he does not have the "authority" to do so.In a video posted on social media, Mamdani instead called on US officials to act on the International Criminal Court's arrest warrant for the Israeli Prime Minister over alleged war crimes. Technically, if Netanyahu set foot in an ICC member state, he could be arrested and handed over to the court - but the US is not a member of the ICC. President Donald Trump has said Netanyahu would not be "arrested, in any way, shape or form" while in the US."He is fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran, which recently killed 52,000 innocent protestors [sic], and has spent the last 47 years killing American Soldiers, and others," Trump said in a Truth Social post on Monday.Trump was responding to comments Mamdani made in an interview with the New York Times on Saturday, in which the mayor said he was having "an active conversation" with law enforcement about the possibility of enforcing the ICC arrest warrant.But on Tuesday, Mamdani said his team had reviewed "every avenue available" under the law, and concluded that "we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this"."The federal government, however, does," Mamdani stated. "And I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant."Mamdani said in the social media video that Netanyahu was "not welcome" in New  York City, where he is expected to travel in September for the UN General Assembly.The ICC issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu in 2024 over alleged war crimes in Gaza, while also issuing an arrest warrant for a Hamas commander.The ICC has the authority to prosecute those accused of genocide, crimes against humanity and war crimes on the territory of states party to the Rome Statute, its founding treaty. During his campaign for New York City mayor, Mamdani repeatedly called Netanyahu a war criminal, and the war in Gaza a genocide. He also said he would order police to arrest Netanyahu if he arrived in the city.Speaking then to the New York Times as a candidate, he said: "This is something that I intend to fulfill." He said that such an arrest would be honouring a warrant issued by the ICC.His remarks come as Democratic voters' support for Israel appears to be shifting, with a recent poll , externalsuggesting 58% of Democrats believe the US is too supportive of Israel, up from 45% in 2024. In response to Mamdani's interview, Danny Danon, the Israeli ambassador to the UN said to the mayor: "You were elected to serve New Yorkers, not Hamas' propaganda."

schema.headline: Mamdani backs off  pledge to arrest Netanyahu citing lack of authority

title: Wreckage of Pan Am plane that shaped aviation safety found 74 years on
description: Figure caption, Watch: Moment research team gets first look at Pan Am plane wreckage after 74 yearsByZahra FatimaThe wreckage of a Pan American Airway
body: Figure caption, Watch: Moment research team gets first look at Pan Am plane wreckage after 74 yearsByZahra FatimaThe wreckage of a Pan American Airways plane has been found 74 years after it plunged into the Atlantic Ocean in a crash that prompted mandatory airline safety briefings.The Clipper Endeavor was found 2,000ft (610m) below sea level off the coast of Puerto Rico with a sonar-equipped drone. It went down on 11 April 1952 following multiple-engine failure shortly after take-off.Everyone onboard survived the impact - but passengers struggled to locate life vests and rafts as the plane rapidly sank. Of the 69 passengers and crew onboard, just 17 survived.The disaster led to sweeping reforms in aviation safety, including compulsory pre-flight safety briefings on every commercial flight.Image source, Air/Sea Heritage Foundation and Deep Sea VisionImage caption, Wreckage of the Clipper Endeavour was discovered more than seven decades after the aircraft disappearedThe expedition to find the wreckage was led by the Air/Sea Heritage Foundation in partnership with the Discovery Channel's Expedition Unknown show and several other organisations.Last month, explorers discovered the ruins of the Douglas DC-4 aircraft using an autonomous sonar drone that detected the aircraft's fuselage and tail. "We are all stunned and elated ​by this ​discovery, yet also humbled ⁠to remember what happened in that place so long ago," said Russ Matthews, president of ​the Air/Sea Heritage Foundation.The plane had been carrying 64 passengers and five crew to New York when the pilot was forced to make a violent water landing. The confusion that ensued became a "catalyst" for modern regulations mandating better flotation equipment on planes and preflight briefings on how to use it, aviation safety expert John Cox told AP.Image source, Pan Am Historical FoundationImage caption, The Clipper Endeavor sank minutes after a hard water landing, killing 52Today, before every commercial flight, cabin crew are required to outline where a plane's exits are, as well as the location of life vests and how to inflate them."Every time you step onboard an airplane today, you are safer because of what happened to Clipper Endeavor and her passengers three-quarters of a century ago," said Expedition Unknown host Josh Gates.Matthews, a maritime explorer and historian, first became interested in the crash in 2019, after reading about a luggage tag from the flight that had washed ashore in Florida.He said in a statement that he had been "astonished that such an important story had largely faded from public memory".He then began piecing together the aircraft's final moments using reports from the Civil Aeronautics Board, the predecessor to the US Federal Aviation Administration.Image source, Discovery ChannelImage caption, Russ Matthews (left) said he had been determined to find the wreckage before the 75th anniversary of the crashThe breakthrough came after researchers uncovered records from a public hearing in Puerto Rico, including a map drawn by air force pilots who had witnessed the crash, which helped narrow down the search area.Researchers used historical records and weather data from the time of the crash to identify the 10 square nautical miles of Atlantic Ocean seabed where the wreckage was likely resting.An attempt to locate the wreckage in 2024 was abandoned due to poor weather - but Matthews said his team had been determined to return before the 75th anniversary of the crash.A drone found the wreckage - which had broken into two sections - during its first pass of the search area on 2 June.A follow-up photographic survey confirmed the wreckage was of the Clipper Endeavor, providing images that revealed the Pan Am logo and aircraft's name were still visible on the fuselage.Image source, Air/Sea Heritage Foundation and Deep Sea VisionImage caption, A photographic survey revealed the Pan Am logo and aircraft's name were still visible"To have a front-row seat to an expedition like this as it's unfolding is tremendous, and so I feel really lucky that we were able to be a part of it," said Gates, who was aboard the surface ship when the wreckage appeared on screen.The discovery could offer fresh hope for the families of passengers of other planes that are presumed to have crashed in the ocean, such as Malaysia Airlines Flight 370.It disappeared 12 years ago with 269 people onboard and, despite numerous searches in the Indian Ocean, has not yet been located.The discovery of the Clipper Endeavor will be featured in a special episode of Expedition Unknown this Autumn.

schema.headline: Wreckage of Pan Am plane that shaped aviation safety found 74 years on
